cards.q: Does intact normal skin resist bacterial infection?
cards.a: Yes — bacteria exist in the external environment, but intact normal skin has a strong resistance to bacterial multiplication and invasion.

cards.q: List the first four factors that enhance skin resistance to bacteria.
cards.a: Relative dryness of normal skin; low skin pH of 5.5; unsaturated long chain fatty acids in skin lipids; and natural antibacterial substances in the sebum.

cards.q: List the last four factors that enhance skin resistance to bacteria.
cards.a: Circulating immunoglobulins; cellular immunity; delayed hypersensitivity; and bacterial interference.

cards.q: What is bacterial interference?
cards.a: The suppression of bacterial species or strains by other bacteria.

cards.q: What is the normal pH of skin?
List the nine factors that favour bacterial skin infections.
cards.a: Trauma (abrasions, scratches, cuts); insect bites; increased humidity; malnutrition; severe illness; low standards of personal or community hygiene; coexisting skin disorders such as atopic dermatitis; natural or acquired immune depression; and iatrogenic immune suppression.

cards.q: Name the six common bacterial skin infections in this lecture.
cards.a: Folliculitis, furunculosis, carbuncles, impetigo, cellulitis, and staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome.

cards.q: Define folliculitis.
cards.a: The superficial inflammation and infection of a single hair follicle, appearing as small, itchy, pus-filled bumps.

cards.q: List the four aetiological categories of folliculitis.
cards.a: Bacterial, fungal, viral, and parasitic.

cards.q: Describe the appearance of bacterial folliculitis.
cards.a: Small, tender, red or pus-filled bumps clustered around hair follicles; each bump resembles a small pimple with a hair sticking out of the centre, surrounded by inflamed, red and warm skin.

cards.q: Where does bacterial folliculitis most commonly appear?
cards.a: On the face, scalp, thighs, or buttocks.

cards.q: Give the treatment of bacterial folliculitis.
cards.a: Warm compresses; antibacterial washes such as benzoyl peroxide; topical antibiotics such as mupirocin or clindamycin; oral antibiotics such as cephalexin or doxycycline in severe or stubborn cases; avoid shaving, picking the bumps and tight clothing; and good personal hygiene.

cards.q: Define furunculosis.
cards.a: The condition of having multiple, deeper and more severe infections of the hair follicles, known as boils (furuncles), which extend into subcutaneous tissue.

cards.q: What is the causative agent of furunculosis, and how does it gain entry?
cards.a: Usually S. aureus, which penetrates abraded perifollicular skin.

cards.q: How may furuncles originate?
cards.a: From a preceding folliculitis, or they may arise initially as a deep-seated, tender, erythematous, perifollicular nodule.

cards.q: State the progression of a furuncle.
cards.a: Indurated lesion → central necrosis → suppuration → rupture and discharge of a central core of necrotic tissue → destruction of the follicle.

cards.q: How does a furuncle heal?
cards.a: With scar formation.

cards.q: List the sites of predilection for furuncles.
cards.a: The hair-bearing areas — scalp, face, back of neck, axillae, buttocks, and groin.

cards.q: List the five predisposing factors to furuncle formation.
cards.a: Obesity, hyperhidrosis, maceration, friction, and pre-existing dermatitis.

cards.q: Give the treatment of furunculosis.
cards.a: Antibiotics, personal hygiene, hot moist compress, and incision and drainage.

cards.q: What organism causes carbuncles?
cards.a: Staphylococcus aureus.

cards.q: Define a carbuncle.
cards.a: An infection of a group of contiguous follicles, with multiple drainage points, accompanied by inflammatory changes in the surrounding connective tissue.

cards.q: Describe the lesions of a carbuncle.
cards.a: Painful, tender, firm to hard, indurated lumps.

cards.q: What systemic features may accompany a carbuncle?
cards.a: Fever, leukocytosis, and bacteraemia.

cards.q: List the sites of predilection for carbuncles.
cards.a: Back of neck, shoulder, hip, and thigh.

cards.q: Give the antibiotic treatment of carbuncles.
cards.a: Oral or parenteral antibiotics such as cloxacillin or cephalexin; cephalosporin, clindamycin or erythromycin for penicillin-allergic patients; and rifampicin or cotrimoxazole in MRSA. Plus attention to personal hygiene.

cards.q: Define impetigo.
cards.a: A contagious bacterial infection of the epidermis.

cards.q: How is impetigo transmitted?
cards.a: By direct contact with infected persons or fomites.

cards.q: Which regions does impetigo most commonly affect, and at what age?
cards.a: Any skin region, but most commonly the face around the nose, mouth and hand; commonly affects children aged 4–7 years.

cards.q: Distinguish primary from secondary impetigo.
cards.a: Primary impetigo is more common in children, with infection through minor breaks in the skin; secondary impetigo occurs at any age, on secondary infection of trauma or wounds.

cards.q: How does the honey-coloured crust of impetigo form?
cards.a: Superficial blisters rupture easily, releasing yellow exudate that dries and forms a honey-coloured crust.

cards.q: How is impetigo diagnosed?
cards.a: Clinically — but it can be confirmed by gram stain or culture of crust or fluid from the bullae.

cards.q: What organism always causes bullous impetigo?
cards.a: S. aureus phage group 2.

cards.q: On what type of skin does bullous impetigo develop, and what are the lesions?
cards.a: On intact skin; vesicles and flaccid transparent bullae containing clear yellow or slightly turbid fluid, with or without surrounding erythema.

cards.q: Where does bullous impetigo occur, and in whom?
cards.a: More on the face, buttocks, trunk, perineum and extremities; common in neonates and children under 5 years, and systemic symptoms are common.

cards.q: Which organisms cause non-bullous impetigo, and which sites does it affect?
cards.a: Usually S. aureus or S. pyogenes, or mixed infection; it typically affects trauma sites.

cards.q: Describe the lesions of non-bullous impetigo.
cards.a: Scattered discrete 1–3 cm lesions with honey-coloured crust and surrounding erythema, most commonly around the mouth and nose; patients may have lymphadenopathy.

cards.q: Which type of impetigo is more common?
cards.a: Non-bullous impetigo.

cards.q: List the predisposing lesions for impetigo.
cards.a: Insect bites, abrasions, lacerations, chickenpox, scabies, pediculosis, and burns.

cards.q: How may impetigo spread to other parts of the body?
cards.a: By the fingers, clothing, and towels.

cards.q: How are local impetigo infections treated?
cards.a: With topical saline or aluminium acetate, then 2% mupirocin ointment.

cards.q: What helps remove the crusts in impetigo?
cards.a: Normal saline or potassium permanganate soaks.

cards.q: Which topical antibiotics are used in impetigo?
cards.a: Mupirocin ointment or fusidic acid.

cards.q: What is used if systemic symptoms are present in impetigo?
cards.a: Cloxacillin or a beta-lactamase resistant antibiotic, e.g. cephalexin.

cards.q: What is used if impetigo is due to MRSA?
cards.a: Clindamycin.

cards.q: Define cellulitis.
cards.a: A serious bacterial skin infection that affects the deeper layers of the skin and underlying tissues.

cards.q: When does cellulitis occur, and what does it look like?
cards.a: When bacteria normally found on the skin surface breach the protective barrier; it appears as a painful, red, swollen and warm area that can spread quickly.

cards.q: Which organisms primarily cause cellulitis?
cards.a: Staphylococcus (staph) or Streptococcus (strep) bacteria, entering through a break in the skin.

cards.q: List the four categories of predisposing and risk factors for cellulitis.
cards.a: Skin injuries (cuts, surgical wounds, insect bites, tattoos, piercings); pre-existing skin conditions (eczema, athlete's foot, psoriasis); weakened immune system (diabetes, HIV, immunosuppressant medications); and poor circulation (lymphoedema, obesity).

cards.q: List the clinical features of cellulitis.
cards.a: Redness and swelling of the skin; pain or tenderness to touch; a feeling of warmth in the affected area; and blisters, fever or swollen lymph nodes in severe cases.

cards.q: Give the treatment of cellulitis.
cards.a: Oral and IV antibiotics targeting Streptococcus and Staphylococcus; wound care and drainage, keeping open wounds clean and covered; elevation above the level of the heart and rest; and pain management with analgesics such as ibuprofen or paracetamol.

cards.q: Why is the affected limb elevated in cellulitis?
cards.a: Resting and elevating it above the level of the heart reduces swelling and improves blood flow.

cards.q: List the complications of cellulitis.
cards.a: Bacteraemia, endocarditis, osteomyelitis, suppurative arthritis, and thrombophlebitis.

cards.q: What causes staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, and what is its other name?
cards.a: Exfoliative toxins A and B produced by Staphylococcus aureus belonging to phage group B; also called Ritter's disease.

cards.q: Describe the erythema of SSSS and its distribution.
cards.a: Scarlatiniform erythema develops diffusely, with a predilection for flexural and periorificial areas.

cards.q: Describe the blisters of SSSS.
cards.a: Extensive superficial sterile flaccid blisters or bullae, which become deroofed revealing large areas of raw red moist skin similar to a hot water burn.

cards.q: What features may occur prior to the onset of the rash in SSSS?
cards.a: Fever, rhinitis, conjunctivitis, irritability, malaise, poor feeding, and exquisite tenderness.

cards.q: What facial features are characteristically prominent in SSSS?
cards.a: Circumpolar erythema, and radial crusting and fissuring around the eyes, mouth and nose; the child may appear toxic.

cards.q: What is Nikolsky's sign, and is it positive in SSSS?
cards.a: Separation of areas of epidermis in response to shearing pressure on the skin — and it is positive in SSSS.

cards.q: Give the treatment of SSSS.
cards.a: Antibiotics such as flucloxacillin or erythromycin; analgesics; application of an emollient and cleaning with isotonic saline; and correction of fluid and electrolyte balance.
